CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression from introduction through method (ID-CNNs) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated up front. Notation and definitions are presented consistently for the iterative dilated convolutional architecture, and related work coverage is thorough, which aids understanding. Figures/tables and examples are generally effective and the overall writing quality is good, though the manuscript would benefit from a few more concrete experimental details (e.g., hyperparameter reporting) and minor clarifications around some comparison choices. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and communicates its approach clearly: the intro, method (ID-CNN architecture and iterative block application), experiments (CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes) and conclusion follow a logical flow. The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is good with consistent notation and clear definitions of the model and evaluation setup. Figures/tables and examples appear effective for conveying results and comparisons against Bi-LSTM baselines and variants, though the paper could improve practical reproducibility by reporting hyperparameters and training details more explicitly.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear progression from introduction through method (ID-CNNs) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated up front. Notation and definitions are presented consistently for the iterative dilated convolutional architecture, and related work coverage is thorough, which aids understanding. Figures/tables and examples are generally effective and the overall writing quality is good, though the manuscript would benefit from a few more concrete experimental details (e.g., hyperparameter reporting) and minor clarifications around some comparison choices.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and communicates its approach clearly: the intro, method (ID-CNN architecture and iterative block application), experiments (CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes) and conclusion follow a logical flow. The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is good with consistent notation and clear definitions of the model and evaluation setup. Figures/tables and examples appear effective for conveying results and comparisons against Bi-LSTM baselines and variants, though the paper could improve practical reproducibility by reporting hyperparameters and training details more explicitly.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses core NLP sequence labeling problems (NER) using standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) and evaluation metrics (segment-level micro F1), and compares against appropriate baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, prior lexicalized models). The proposed model (ID-CNNs) and experiments—including document-context variants and structured decoding—are squarely aimed at the readership of top NLP/ML venues interested in efficient sequence models. While some engineering details (e.g., hyperparameter reporting, code availability) are not mentioned in the summary, the topic, datasets, and evaluation protocol place the work squarely within the conference scope. | B(5): This paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core sequence-labeling problem (NER) with a practically motivated model that prioritizes wall-clock speed and GPU-parallelizability (ID-CNNs), evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) and compares to relevant baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, CNN variants). The empirical focus on throughput-friendly architecture, document-level context experiments, and clear discussion of when greedy decoding lags Viterbi make it directly relevant to researchers and practitioners concerned with scalable, real-world sequence labeling. The methodology and evaluation meet community standards and the positioning/related work coverage are appropriate for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses core NLP sequence labeling problems (NER) using standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) and evaluation metrics (segment-level micro F1), and compares against appropriate baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, prior lexicalized models). The proposed model (ID-CNNs) and experiments—including document-context variants and structured decoding—are squarely aimed at the readership of top NLP/ML venues interested in efficient sequence models. While some engineering details (e.g., hyperparameter reporting, code availability) are not mentioned in the summary, the topic, datasets, and evaluation protocol place the work squarely within the conference scope.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core sequence-labeling problem (NER) with a practically motivated model that prioritizes wall-clock speed and GPU-parallelizability (ID-CNNs), evaluates on standard benchmarks (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) and compares to relevant baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, CNN variants). The empirical focus on throughput-friendly architecture, document-level context experiments, and clear discussion of when greedy decoding lags Viterbi make it directly relevant to researchers and practitioners concerned with scalable, real-world sequence labeling. The methodology and evaluation meet community standards and the positioning/related work coverage are appropriate for the venue.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares to strong and relevant baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, multiple CNN variants, and classic lexicalized models including Durrett & Klein) on the same datasets (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) using standard F1, and it explicitly acknowledges when prior methods (Bi-LSTM-CRF) outperform their greedy model. However, fairness is weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and no mention of reproducible code or multiple seeds; claims of superior speed are not supported by careful wall‑clock timing under matched conditions. The authors also decline to compare deeper/well-tuned LSTM/CNN alternatives (citing speed/overfitting) rather than empirically ruling them out, so important baselines are somewhat underexplored. | B(4): The paper compares ID-CNNs to appropriate and standard baselines (greedy Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, non-dilated CNNs, and classic lexicalized models) on the same datasets (CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes) using segment-level micro F1, and it explicitly acknowledges when prior methods (Bi-LSTM-CRF) outperform on OntoNotes. Differences are analyzed (greedy vs. CRF decoding, effect of document context) and related work is discussed. Minor omissions include lack of comparison to stronger/deeper recent sequence models and limited reporting of hyperparameters/code, but the comparisons shown are generally fair and relevant for the paper’s practical speed/accuracy claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to strong and relevant baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, multiple CNN variants, and classic lexicalized models including Durrett & Klein) on the same datasets (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) using standard F1, and it explicitly acknowledges when prior methods (Bi-LSTM-CRF) outperform their greedy model. However, fairness is weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and no mention of reproducible code or multiple seeds; claims of superior speed are not supported by careful wall‑clock timing under matched conditions. The authors also decline to compare deeper/well-tuned LSTM/CNN alternatives (citing speed/overfitting) rather than empirically ruling them out, so important baselines are somewhat underexplored.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares ID-CNNs to appropriate and standard baselines (greedy Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, non-dilated CNNs, and classic lexicalized models) on the same datasets (CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes) using segment-level micro F1, and it explicitly acknowledges when prior methods (Bi-LSTM-CRF) outperform on OntoNotes. Differences are analyzed (greedy vs. CRF decoding, effect of document context) and related work is discussed. Minor omissions include lack of comparison to stronger/deeper recent sequence models and limited reporting of hyperparameters/code, but the comparisons shown are generally fair and relevant for the paper’s practical speed/accuracy claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is not a radical theoretical advance but presents a meaningful, well-executed engineering contribution: applying dilated convolutions with an iterative-block design to sequence labeling yields practical speed and accuracy benefits. Given that the idea adapts known primitives in a non-trivial way that clearly differentiates from prior CNN and Bi-LSTM approaches and has useful empirical impact, a score of 4 reflects solid originality as an impactful, practitioner-oriented novelty rather than purely incremental or purely foundational work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies dilated convolutions and an iterative-block scheme to sequence labeling, which is a sensible and practically useful composition but does not constitute a fundamentally new modeling paradigm—dilated convolutions and iterative application of blocks are known primitives. The authors do clearly differentiate their ID-CNN design from Bi-LSTMs, CRFs, and standard CNN baselines and demonstrate empirical benefits (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes), but the technical novelty is an engineering combination rather than a novel theoretical insight or principle. Overall the contribution is an incremental yet well-motivated advance that would be of interest to practitioners but is unlikely to surprise a theory-focused expert.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies dilated convolutions in a novel, practical way to token-level sequence labeling (ID-CNNs) and introduces iterative application of a dilation block to extend receptive field without extra parameters—an architecture-level trick that is clearly differentiated from Bi-LSTM and prior CNN baselines and emphasizes GPU-parallelism and runtime benefits. While dilated convolutions themselves are known from other domains, using them plus the iterative-block design for fast, wide-context NER and showing competitive accuracy with much higher throughput is a notable, non-trivial adaptation likely to inform practitioners. The work is not a revolutionary theoretical advance, but it is a meaningful, well-justified engineering contribution with clear novelty over standard sequence-labeling models.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a useful practical contribution by enabling fast, parallelizable sequence labeling with competitive accuracy, and is likely to influence engineering practice. It is not a transformative theoretical advance and has some dataset/benchmark limitations (and is outperformed by BiLSTM‑CRF in some settings), but the clear empirical speed/accuracy tradeoff and applicability to production systems justify a slightly above‑average impact score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem (fast, accurate sequence labeling) and demonstrates that iterated dilated convolutions (ID-CNNs) can deliver competitive NER accuracy while enabling much more parallel GPU computation than recurrent models, which is a useful empirical contribution. However, from a theory‑purist perspective the advance is largely an engineering/compositional application of dilated convolutions and iterative application without formal analysis of why iteration or dilation yield the observed gains; results are dataset‑limited (CoNLL, OntoNotes) and the greedy variant is still outperformed by BiLSTM‑CRF on OntoNotes. Consequently the work is likely to be a practically useful, moderately influential direction (encouraging more parallel sequence models), but it falls short of a transformative conceptual or theoretical contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—fast, parallelizable sequence labeling—by adapting dilated convolutions into an efficient ID-CNN architecture that yields competitive NER accuracy while enabling much greater GPU parallelism and throughput than recurrent models. The demonstrated speed/accuracy tradeoffs on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes, plus the ability to incorporate document context without extra parameters, make it likely practitioners will adopt or build on the approach for production systems. It isn’t fully transformative (e.g., BiLSTM-CRF still outperforms on some settings), but it offers a strong, scalable alternative that opens useful engineering directions for high-throughput sequence labeling.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree that the empirical results support the main claims about ID-CNNs’ speed and competitive accuracy, but that the paper lacks important practical details (hyperparameters, random seeds, statistical tests) and some ablations/comparisons to fully isolate contributions. These omissions weaken reproducibility and the strength of marginal claims but do not overturn the core experimental findings, so a slightly above-average score reflects overall correctness with notable reproducibility gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main empirical claims (ID-CNNs provide fast, competitive NER and scale to document context) are supported by experiments on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes, but the technical evidence is incomplete: no mathematical analysis or proofs are provided (acceptable for an empirical architecture paper but noted) and no hyperparameter reporting, random-seed variability, or statistical significance tests are mentioned. The experimental methodology uses reasonable baselines (Bi-LSTM variants, CNN baselines, prior lexicalized systems), but I penalize the work for not comparing to better-tuned or deeper sequence models and for omitting ablations/statistics that would isolate each claimed contribution (iterated blocks, dilation, document context). There are some unsupported generalizations (e.g., claims about massive parallelism and being the first to use dilated convs for sequence labeling) and the limitations on OntoNotes indicate the conclusions are not uniformly robust across datasets.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims (ID-CNNs enable wide-context token predictions with strong speed/parallelization and competitive accuracy) are supported by experimental comparisons to sensible baselines (Bi-LSTM variants, non-dilated CNNs) on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes, and limitations are honestly reported. There are no formal derivations to audit, and the methodology is generally sound, but important practical details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting or code availability mentioned, and no statistical significance testing), which weakens reproducibility and confidence in marginal gains. Overall the technical approach and results appear correct and convincing, with only minor issues that don’t overturn the main conclusions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental evaluation is adequate with meaningful baselines and useful ablations, and the paper candidly discusses limitations. However, reproducibility is weakened by missing hyperparameter details, random-seed/statistical-significance analysis, and concrete runtime/throughput information to support speed claims; these issues justify a slight downgrade but not a rejection, so I assign a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental evaluation is adequate: two standard NER corpora (CoNLL-2003, OntoNotes) are used and comparisons include Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, several CNN baselines and prior lexicalized systems. However, important sensitivity analyses are missing or weak: hyperparameters are not reported, random-seed variability and statistical significance are not discussed, and wall-clock speed/equipment details for the parallel-GPU claims are not provided. The paper does include some ablations (noshare, 1-loss variants) and discusses limitations (performance gap to Bi-LSTM-CRF on OntoNotes), and related work coverage appears thorough, but reproducibility details are insufficient for full verification.

reviewer_b.justification: Experiments are fairly comprehensive: evaluations on CoNLL-2003 and OntoNotes with comparisons to strong baselines (Bi-LSTM, Bi-LSTM-CRF, non-dilated CNNs, prior lexicalized models) and document-context experiments support the main claims. Appropriate ablations/sensitivity checks are present (ID-CNN variants like noshare and 1-loss, non-dilated CNN baselines, and impact of document context). Related work coverage is reported as thorough and limitations (performance gaps vs. Bi-LSTM-CRF on OntoNotes and when extra context hurts) are discussed. Reproducibility is weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and no mention of released code or detailed runtime/throughput figures to substantiate the strong speed/parallelism claims, so I downgrade slightly.

main_claims: Introduce Iterated Dilated Convolutional Neural Networks (ID-CNNs) applying dilated convolutions to sequence labeling, claiming to be the first to use dilated convolutions for sequence labeling.

ID-CNNs efficiently aggregate wide (sentence- and document-level) context via stacked dilated convolutions with exponentially increasing dilation and iterative application of blocks, producing fast and accurate token-level predictions.

ID-CNNs outperform comparable greedy Bi-LSTM baselines and other greedy methods on NER benchmarks, and improve when incorporating document-level context; they provide competitive accuracy while enabling massively parallel GPU computation.
_summary.methodology: Stack dilated convolutional layers with exponentially increasing dilation in a block B, then iteratively apply B multiple times (introducing no extra parameters) to produce per-token scores; optionally combine with CRF decoding for structured prediction.
baselines: Ratinov and Roth (2009) greedy lexicalized model
limitations: Greedy ID-CNNs are out-performed by Bi-LSTM-CRF Viterbi-decoding models on OntoNotes; the gap between greedy and Viterbi-decoding models is wider on OntoNotes than on CoNLL-2003.
Deeper or more elaborate CNN/LSTM architectures are not compared because they are slower, tend to overfit on the relatively small datasets, and/or do not maintain token-level resolution.
Adding wider document context can decrease performance for some models (e.g., Bi-LSTM-CRF) on OntoNotes, likely due to added noise for high-scoring models.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - sequence labeling / Named Entity Recognition (NER)

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Professor of machine learning with a track record of formal analyses of neural architectures and sequence models.
traits: penalises papers that present primarily empirical/engineering improvements without theoretical analysis or formal justification of why the method works (e.g., no complexity bounds, no convergence/expressivity claims)
demands clear, novel conceptual contributions rather than re-application of known components; downgrades work that composes existing modules (dilated convs, stacking, iteration) without new insights
requires rigorous experimental protocol: multiple seeds, statistical significance tests, and ablation isolating each claimed contribution (will deduct points if missing)
expects comparisons to well-tuned baselines (including optimized implementations) and will penalise speed claims that do not measure wall-clock time under fair conditions
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on formal clarity and principled novelty, giving credit only when a paper provides theoretical insight or rigorous empirical substantiation of a claimed conceptual advance.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Systems Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher/engineer in applied NLP and ML systems, specializing in scalable models and deployment of sequence-labeling systems.
traits: rewards papers that demonstrate meaningful wall-clock speedups and GPU-parallelizability on standard tasks (will upscore if runtime and throughput gains are convincing)
favors simple, easily-implemented architectures that yield strong empirical gains and good accuracy/runtime tradeoffs (prefers practical impact over formal guarantees)

values thorough empirical evaluation on standard benchmarks and realistic settings (document-level context, production-oriented metrics); will upscore for strong benchmark performance and useful ablations
penalises overly complex solutions with many moving parts; prefers approaches that reduce latency or allow massive batching
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I prioritize practical impact and reproducible empirical gains: I reward simple, scalable methods that improve speed/accuracy tradeoffs and are likely to be useful in real-world systems.
